attribution: Question set unifies OWASP AISVS v1.0 (CC-BY-SA-4.0, © OWASP Foundation, https://github.com/OWASP/AISVS), ISO/IEC 42001:2023 (clause references only — © ISO/IEC), and the EU AI Act / Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 (public domain). Questions are paraphrased references, not verbatim standard text.
disclaimer: This is a self-assessment aid, NOT legal advice. EU AI Act risk classification and fundamental-rights determinations are legal calls owned by your counsel.

questions.text: Do you maintain an inventory of all AI systems your organization develops, deploys, or procures?
Have you assigned clear ownership and accountability for AI systems (e.g. an AI governance lead or responsible person)?
Do you have a documented AI policy defining acceptable use, risk tolerance, and ethical principles for AI?
Do staff who build or operate AI systems receive AI-specific risk and security training?
Have you classified each AI system by its EU AI Act risk tier (prohibited / high-risk / limited / minimal)?
Do you perform a risk assessment before deploying a new AI system or a significant model change?
For high-risk AI systems, have you conducted a fundamental rights impact assessment (FRIA)?
Do you assess and document the potential for bias, discrimination, or harmful outcomes in your AI systems?
Have you verified that none of your AI systems engage in EU AI Act prohibited practices (e.g. social scoring, manipulative techniques, prohibited biometric categorization)?
Do you maintain traceability of the data used to train or fine-tune your models (provenance, lineage, version)?
Do you assess training/input data for quality, representativeness, and presence of sensitive or personal data?
For third-party or open-source models you use, do you verify their provenance, integrity, and known vulnerabilities?
Do you maintain a bill of materials (SBOM/MBOM) for the AI/model components in your systems?
When procuring AI from a vendor, do you assess the vendor's AI security posture against a recognized standard?
Do you validate and sanitize inputs to your AI systems to defend against prompt injection and adversarial inputs?
Do you validate and constrain AI-generated outputs before they are used or shown (schema, range, safety checks)?
Have you implemented guardrails to prevent your AI from producing harmful, biased, or unsafe outputs?
Have you tested your AI systems against adversarial attacks (prompt injection, evasion, data poisoning, model extraction)?
For AI that interacts with people, do you clearly disclose AI use and label AI-generated content / deepfakes?
Do you enforce access controls and authentication for AI components (models, training pipelines, inference endpoints)?
Are your AI infrastructure and deployment configs hardened and reviewed (secrets, network isolation, least privilege)?
If you use vector databases, embeddings, or retrieval (RAG), do you secure them against unauthorized access and injection?
If you use AI agents, tool-use, or the Model Context Protocol (MCP), do you constrain and monitor what actions the AI can take?
For high-risk AI systems, have you implemented meaningful human oversight (a human can review, override, or stop the system)?
Do you maintain technical documentation describing how each AI system works, its intended purpose, and its limitations?
Do you keep records/logs that let you trace and explain AI system decisions (automatic logging)?
Do you continuously monitor production AI systems for anomalies, drift, performance degradation, and abuse?
Do you have a process to detect, respond to, and report serious AI incidents or malfunctions?
Do you periodically re-assess your AI systems against changing risks, regulations, and model updates?
Do you run a measurable AI governance improvement process (metrics, internal audits, corrective actions)?
